current_fact.fact_text: When cosmonaut Valeri Polyakov returned to Earth in 1995, he had been in orbit for 438 days straight—the longest continuous human spaceflight ever. His mission turned his own body into a living experiment on whether humans could physically and psychologically endure a journey as long as a trip to Mars.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

current_fact.match_reason: Polyakov’s long, patient orbit around Earth echoes the reflective, slow-motion mood of "Slow Planet," both about stretching time and adapting to extended journeys.

queue.fact_text: On March 22, 1960, Arthur Schawlow and Charles Townes received the first patent for a laser—essentially a machine that makes light march in perfect lockstep. Lasers, once a science-fiction idea, now quietly run our barcode scanners, fiber-optic internet, eye surgeries, and even some of the instruments that measure the universe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: The invention of the laser is literally a story about harnessing light and electricity with shocking precision, fitting an episode themed around electric energy and jolts of power.

queue.fact_text: In 1996, Space Shuttle Atlantis launched on STS-76 to rendezvous with the Russian space station Mir, turning a Cold War rivalry into literal docked collaboration. Astronaut Shannon Lucid began what would become a record-breaking stay on Mir, transforming the shuttle into a shuttle between former adversaries.

episode.title: Morning Drops #32 - A Weekend In London
episode.description: Recorded in London, marking a weekend reunion between Gaia and Yasmin. A selection inspired by London's energy, seen through the eyes of two inseparable friends — music from concerts, club nights, and long walks across town. Four tracks to ease you into the week at the right pace.
songs: Jimmy Castor Bunch - It's Just Begun
The Asphodells - Late Flowering Lust (Phil Kieran Remix)

queue.match_reason: A mission built on international rendezvous and cooperation pairs with an episode about reunion and shared journeys through a global city’s mixed cultures.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1963, the Beatles released their debut album "Please Please Me," recorded astonishingly in a single marathon day at Abbey Road Studios. What began as a rough, live-in-the-room session became the spark that helped transform a local Liverpool band into a force that rewired global pop culture.

episode.title: Morning Drops #01 - Timeless Legacy
episode.description: Gaia guides us through timeless music to start the morning with the right foot! The first episode of the Morning Drops series — classic sounds that never fade.
songs: Roberta Flack - Killing me softly with his song

queue.match_reason: A foundational, timeless debut album resonates with an episode explicitly about music that forms a 'timeless legacy' and still shapes mornings decades later.

queue.fact_text: In 1957, a U.S. Air Force Boeing C-97 Stratofreighter vanished over the Pacific Ocean with 67 people on board, leaving no definitive trace. The disappearance sits among a haunting category of aviation mysteries where vast ocean and thin air easily erase human footprints.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: An aircraft lost to the ocean’s vastness pairs with an episode that enters deep, remote waters; both meditate on how sea and sky can swallow human stories into quiet mystery.

queue.fact_text: Back in 1508, Ferdinand II of Aragon appointed navigator Amerigo Vespucci as chief navigator of the Spanish Empire, essentially making him the GPS of one of the world’s rising sea powers. Vespucci’s meticulous star-guided mapping and letters helped Europeans realize that the lands across the Atlantic were not Asia, but an entirely separate set of continents—later bearing his name.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: A royal appointment to guide ocean voyages matches an episode literally set at sea, both centered on navigation, long journeys, and the stories that ride on the waves.

queue.fact_text: In 1997, Comet Hale–Bopp swept past Earth at about 1.3 astronomical units away, so bright that it was visible to the naked eye for a record 18 months. Its icy nucleus, older than our planet, carried ancient material from the early solar system, briefly painting the night sky with a cosmic time capsule.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

queue.match_reason: A spectacular comet lighting up the sky is a literal cosmic event, perfectly matched with an episode themed around "Cosmic" energy and futuristic, space-tinged dance music.

queue.fact_text: On March 22, 1895, the Lumière brothers publicly demonstrated their movie film technology, projecting moving images for an audience who had mostly only known still photographs. To those first viewers, the sight of a train rushing toward the camera or workers leaving a factory felt like magic: reality captured and replayed in light.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: Early cinema and flamenco both evolved into performance arts that fuse movement, emotion, and storytelling; this episode about the history of a visual, dramatic music tradition mirrors the birth of film as moving images.

queue.fact_text: In 1621, the Pilgrims of Plymouth Colony signed a peace treaty with Massasoit, leader of the Wampanoag, with Squanto serving as interpreter. For several decades it held as a fragile bridge between newcomers and the Indigenous people whose land and knowledge made the colonists’ survival possible.

episode.title: Morning Drops #06 - Friend's Home
episode.description: Yasmin and Gaia are reunited under the same roof to spend Easter Monday morning together, exploring that heartwarming 'just like home' feeling that comes from being surrounded by beloved friends.

queue.match_reason: A tentative peace agreement and shared survival gesture toward the idea of building a sense of home with others, resonating with an episode about togetherness and the warmth of being under one roof.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1975, a fire at the Browns Ferry Nuclear Power Plant in Alabama started because workers used a candle to check for air leaks, accidentally igniting cables. The near-disaster led to sweeping changes in nuclear safety rules worldwide, showing how a tiny flame can expose enormous vulnerabilities in complex systems.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: A near-catastrophe that forced new safety and resilience measures fits with an episode about weathering storms and emerging with a more hopeful, careful approach to the future.

queue.fact_text: In 1942’s Second Battle of Sirte, Royal Navy convoys and Italy’s Regia Marina clashed in the Mediterranean Sea under harsh weather and smoke screens. The fight became a grim tactical chess match over lifelines to Malta, where waves, wind, and artillery all conspired to decide who controlled a critical sea corridor.

episode.title: Morning Drops #17 - Steel City
episode.description: Gaia takes us on a short trip through Sheffield's sounds — from electronic textures to raw guitars and sharp storytelling. Four tracks, one city, different edges of the same story.
songs: The Human League - The Sound Of The Crowd

queue.match_reason: A brutal naval clash of metal hulls and industrial warfare echoes the "Steel City" soundscape, where hard-edged synths and guitars tell stories of tension and survival in another industrial landscape.

queue.fact_text: In 1668, the privateer Henry Morgan landed in Cuba to raid Puerto del Príncipe, leading bands of sanctioned pirates deep inland. His attacks blurred the line between outlaw and official warfare, with royal letters of marque turning plunder into policy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: A dangerous Caribbean raid and seaborne journey match an episode about travel and movement, where crossing waters and entering new territories change how we see the world—even if Morgan’s version was far less peaceful.

queue.fact_text: On March 22, 1622, Powhatan warriors launched a coordinated surprise attack on English settlements around Jamestown, killing about a third of the colonists. The event, often called the Indian massacre of 1622, shattered the illusion of inevitable English dominance and triggered a long, brutal cycle of retaliation and war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: A violent uprising rooted in dispossession and colonial expansion aligns with an episode centered on politically charged music about resistance, structural violence, and rage against oppressive systems.

queue.fact_text: In 1993, Intel began shipping the first Pentium processors, tiny silicon slabs etched with millions of transistors that could execute over 100 million instructions per second. This chip family quietly powered early web browsing, home PCs, and a whole generation’s first experiences of the digital world.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: The Pentium era kick-started mass personal computing, leading directly to the smartphone age; an episode about our tangled relationship with digital devices is a natural echo of that first big leap in processing power.

queue.fact_text: The Bostran era, which begins in AD 106, marks the creation of a local calendar in the Roman province of Arabia Petraea after Emperor Trajan’s conquest. It’s a reminder that even timekeeping is political: new rulers often start counting years from the moment their power begins.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

queue.match_reason: A new calendar era defined by conquest mirrors an episode about structuring time and life around disciplined practice and training cycles—another way humans carve up time to give it meaning.

queue.fact_text: On March 22, 2017, during the Syrian civil war, about 500 fighters of the Syrian Democratic Forces were airlifted across the Euphrates by U.S. helicopters to launch the Battle of Tabqa. It was a bold airborne maneuver, leaping over front lines and a historic river to cut off a strategic dam from the so‑called Islamic State.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

queue.match_reason: A high‑risk operation in a brutal war—dense with geopolitics and defiance—parallels an episode dedicated to Residente, whose work is steeped in political critique, resistance, and the human cost of conflict.

queue.fact_text: On March 22, 2019, two buses collided in the early morning on a highway near Kintampo, Ghana, killing at least 50 people. The tragedy highlighted how infrastructure, road safety, and long-distance night travel intersect in many countries, turning routine journeys into high-risk undertakings.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: A tragic crash in Ghana is paired with an episode that celebrates West African musical resilience and creativity, including Ghanaian highlife, acknowledging both the pain and the vibrant cultural heartbeat of the region.

queue.fact_text: On March 22, 2004, Ahmed Yassin, the quadriplegic cleric who co‑founded Hamas, was killed by Israeli air-to-surface missiles as he left a mosque in Gaza. His assassination intensified a long-running cycle of violence and raised fierce debates about targeted killings, resistance, and terrorism.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: A Middle Eastern political assassination with deep regional and global implications is set against an episode whose diverse, reflective selection (including trip-hop and Maghreb influences) invites contemplation about complex, contested realities.

queue.fact_text: On March 22, 2024, attackers stormed Crocus City Hall near Moscow with guns and explosives during a concert, killing at least 145 people and wounding hundreds more. The assault turned a place meant for music and gathering into a site of terror, underlining how even shared cultural spaces can become targets.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A devastating attack on a concert hall calls for a gentle, reflective episode about healing, vulnerability, and slowly rebuilding after sudden trauma, honoring the contrast between music as refuge and music interrupted by violence.

queue.fact_text: NASA’s Space Shuttle Columbia returned to this date not only as a spacecraft, but as part of a broader story of human risk in spaceflight that would later include its tragic loss in 2003. Every shuttle mission, including STS-3, quietly tested the boundaries of how far we’re willing to push fragile machines and fragile bodies in orbit.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: Revisiting NASA as a symbol of boundary‑pushing exploration fits an evening mix that gradually leaves familiar ground and travels across sonic landscapes, echoing the feeling of drifting into the unknown.

queue.fact_text: The Jamestown massacre and the long wars that followed weren’t just isolated episodes of violence—they were part of a slow, relentless reshaping of an entire continent’s human map. Each treaty, raid, and retaliation rewrote who could call which land home, long before anyone wrote national borders on a globe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: The displacement and resistance of Indigenous peoples in North America is poignantly mirrored by Tuareg music born from exile, land, and survival—both telling stories of people whose identities are bound to contested territories.

queue.fact_text: The Lumière brothers’ 1895 film demonstration opened the door for cinema, but it also quietly introduced a new way of remembering: we could replay time, over and over. Stories, gestures, and songs no longer vanished when they ended—they could loop, travel, and survive us.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: Cinema’s birth as moving images parallels trip-hop’s cinematic sampling and mood-driven storytelling—both art forms that feel like films in sound or light, looping memories into new narratives.

queue.fact_text: The laser patent of 1960 didn’t just give us pointers and surgery tools; it eventually led to fiber-optic cables that carry pulses of light under oceans, quietly stitching continents together. Today, much of Earth’s digital music, videos, and calls travel through glass threads lit by descendants of that early idea.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: A precision technology that reshaped how information moves across the world aligns with an episode about Auto-Tune, another subtle tool that quietly but radically changed the way sound—and especially music—travels and is perceived.

published.fact_text: In 1982, Space Shuttle Columbia lifted off on STS-3, a mission that tested the shuttle like a prototype spaceship-car: astronauts deliberately flew it in manual modes, exposed it to extreme heating, and even carried a secret test pallet in the cargo bay. It was only the third shuttle flight in history, yet NASA was already learning how to turn experimental spaceplanes into a reusable fleet.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A literal space shuttle mission pairs with an episode built around the metaphor of "space" as possibility and momentum, capturing the experimental optimism of early shuttle flights.

published.fact_text: When cosmonaut Valeri Polyakov returned to Earth in 1995, he had been in orbit for 438 days straight—the longest continuous human spaceflight ever. His mission turned his own body into a living experiment on whether humans could physically and psychologically endure a journey as long as a trip to Mars.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: Polyakov’s long, patient orbit around Earth echoes the reflective, slow-motion mood of "Slow Planet," both about stretching time and adapting to extended journeys.
